source_title: Essentials: Optimize Your Exercise Program with Science-Based Tools | Jeff Cavaliere

paragraphs.speaker: andrew
paragraphs.name: Andrew Huberman
paragraphs.text: I'm a longtime consumer of your content. I've learned a tremendous amount about fitness, both in the weight room, cardio, nutrition, things that I've applied for over a decade. One of your mantras is, if you wanna look like an athlete, train like an athlete. What's the sort of contour of a basic program that anybody could think about as a starting place?

paragraphs.speaker: jeff
paragraphs.name: Jeff Cavaliere
paragraphs.text: I think it's like a 60-40 split, which would be leaning towards weight training, strength and then the conditioning aspect would be about 40%. So if you look at it over a course of a training week, five days in a gym would be a great task. Three days strength training, Monday, Wednesday, Friday, conditioning Tuesday, Thursday, two days. We try to keep our workouts to an hour or less, if possible. I always say, you can either train long or you can train hard, but you can't do both. As you start to get older, it's the length of the workout that actually causes more problems than the intensity of what you're doing, particularly if you're warmed up properly.

paragraphs.speaker: jeff
paragraphs.name: Jeff Cavaliere
paragraphs.text: For me, the first rule is will you stick to it? I don't particularly like full body splits. A split not done is not effective. So you need to find one that fits. Maybe you go into an alternative option like a push-pull legs. That could be done either one cycle through the week on a Monday, Wednesday, Friday split, or it could be twice in a week. The bro split days, those still work effectively. There's a reason why they worked in the past. That tends to be one of the ones that people like the most, because they can go in, they get their pump, they feel good, it's pretty solely focused on one muscle group.

paragraphs.speaker: jeff
paragraphs.name: Jeff Cavaliere
paragraphs.text: I just like to put it at the end of the workout because you don't wanna in any way compromise the weight training workout. Even if my effort level is lower, my output is lower, if it's still placing a demand on my cardiac output to get that conditioning effect, because I'm fatigued, it still has a demand on my cardiac output. If we could blend function across these realms and not have such a delineation between this is my weight training and this is my conditioning, I always think that you've got a better opportunity to get that more well-rounded result. Some footwork drills, like ladders or line drills. People become intrigued and interested. As we become almost distracted by the challenge, we're now finding ourselves conditioning.

paragraphs.speaker: jeff
paragraphs.name: Jeff Cavaliere
paragraphs.text: During my workouts, even as a young kid just starting out, I wanted to know what was supposed to be doing the work. If the bicep is what's supposed to be doing the work, then I want to make sure that the bicep's doing the work. There's a term I like to call muscularity, which is the level of sort of resting tone in the muscle. If you can learn how to just start to engage that muscle better, the muscularity, the resting tone of that muscle is harder, it's more alive. It's all driven from being able to connect better neurologically with the muscle that you're trying to train. When you're trying to go and create muscle hypertrophy, you need to seek ways to make it feel more uncomfortable. If you don't feel the discomfort, then you're doing something wrong.

paragraphs.speaker: jeff
paragraphs.name: Jeff Cavaliere
paragraphs.text: Grip strength is very, very much tied to performance and recovery. When I was at the Mets, we used to actually take grip strength measurements as a baseline in spring training all the time. We have found that with one of those scales, those old-fashioned bathroom scales, it's a great tool for just squeezing the scale with your hands and seeing what type of output you could get. When you first wake up in the morning, you're still groggy. Try to make a fist as hard as you can. You're gonna sit there angry at your fist because it won't contract as hard as you know it can. When you start to measure that on a daily basis, you can get a pretty good sense of where you're at. When people start to see a drop-off of 10% or so of their grip output, you really should skip the gym that day.

paragraphs.speaker: jeff
paragraphs.name: Jeff Cavaliere
paragraphs.text: The shoulder has the most mobility in the body of any joint, but it's also got the least stability. The only muscle group that actually externally rotates the shoulder is going to be the rotator cuff. Unless you are devoted to training through external rotation, you're not training that function.
